facts.label: COLORS OF THE FLAG
facts.story: Morocco's shirt has carried red and green since the earliest national sides took the field in the 1920s, colors lifted straight from the flag: red for the ruling Alawite dynasty, green for the five-pointed star at its center. Adidas outfitted the Atlas Lions through their breakthrough 1986 run, before Lotto, Puma, Nike and Puma again cycled through the kit deals, yet the red-and-green base has never changed, making it one of the most consistent identities in African football.

facts.label: MOSAIC ON THE SHIRT
facts.story: For the 2022 World Cup, Puma built Morocco's white away shirt around zellige, the hand-cut geometric tilework that decorates fountains, riads and mosques across the country and is studied as much for its mathematics as its beauty. The design turned centuries-old craftsmanship into interlocking stars and polygons across the chest, a deliberate break from generic templates. Worn during the run to the semi-final, it became one of the most photographed shirts of the tournament and sold out at retailers worldwide within days.

1970.headline: Jarir's Historic First Goal

annotations.label: FIRST AFRICAN GOAL AT A WORLD CUP
annotations.story: Houmane Jarir put Morocco ahead in the 21st minute against West Germany in León, becoming the first African player to score at a World Cup. West Germany rallied to win 2–1, but nine days later Morocco held Bulgaria 1–1, the first point an African nation had ever taken at the tournament.

annotations.label: NO BADGE, NO BRAND
annotations.story: The shirt Jarir scored in carried no crest, no stripes, no manufacturer mark — just a deep red cotton weave with a green v-neck and cuffs, stitched for a squad drawn entirely from the domestic league. Commercial kit contracts were still years away; this was pure amateur-era football, built for León's altitude and heat.

annotations.label: COACH OF TWO NATIONS
annotations.story: Blagoje Vidinić, a Yugoslav goalkeeper who'd won Olympic gold in 1960, coached Morocco to its World Cup debut in 1970. Four years later he took the bench for Zaire at the 1974 tournament — the only man to coach two different African nations at the World Cup, a feat still unmatched over five decades on.

1970.homeColors: red shirt, green shorts, red socks

1986.headline: First African Group Winners

annotations.label: FIRST TO WIN A WORLD CUP GROUP
annotations.story: Abderrazak Khairi's brace and Abdelkrim Merry's strike sank Portugal 3–1 in the group finale, sending Morocco top of a group above England and Poland. It made the Atlas Lions the first African or Arab team to win a World Cup group and advance past the first round.

annotations.label: MATTHÄUS'S LATE, LATE WINNER
annotations.story: One minute from full time in the Round of 16, Lothar Matthäus curled a free kick past goalkeeper Badou Zaki to end Morocco's run 1–0. It remains the closest an African team came to reaching the World Cup quarter-finals until 2022.

annotations.label: BADGE BORROWED FROM THE OLYMPICS
annotations.story: Adidas dressed Morocco's Mexico run in red with a wide green polo collar and shoulder trim, typical mid-'80s excess. The crest told its own story: the shirt carried the five interlocking Olympic rings, lent by the Moroccan Olympic Committee since the football federation had not yet issued its own dedicated World Cup badge.

1986.homeColors: red shirt with green collar and shoulder trim, green shorts, red socks (Adidas)
1986.awayColors: green shirt with red collar and shoulder stripes, white shorts (Adidas)

annotations.label: NADER'S FLEETING EQUALIZER
annotations.story: Down 1-0 to the Netherlands in Orlando, Hassan Nader leveled it in the 47th minute, and Morocco stayed level with a side that had reached the semi-finals four years earlier. The tie held until Bryan Roy's 78th-minute strike settled it 2-1 — the closest scoreline of three straight one-goal group defeats.

annotations.label: BORROWED FROM FIORENTINA
annotations.story: Lotto's 1994 kit wasn't built from scratch — it recycled a template designed for Fiorentina, complete with a fading gradient across the chest that shaded from deep red toward pink. A giant federation crest anchored the design, while the matching goalkeeper shirt went further still: tribal-patterned panels in green, purple and yellow.

annotations.label: PLAYING WHERE THEY WANTED TO HOST
annotations.story: Six years earlier, Morocco had bid to host this very tournament, only to lose a single Zurich vote 10-7 to the United States after inspectors flagged the nine stadiums it still needed to build. In 1994 the Atlas Lions arrived anyway, guests in the country that beat their bid, and left without a win.

1994.homeColors: red shirt, white shorts, red socks (Lotto)

1998.headline: Bassir's Statement Win

annotations.label: BIGGEST WIN IN WORLD CUP HISTORY
annotations.story: Salaheddine Bassir scored twice and Abdeljalil Hadda added another as Morocco routed Scotland 3–0 in Saint-Étienne, still the biggest margin of victory in the nation's World Cup history. It came too late — Morocco had already been eliminated after losing to Brazil.

annotations.label: GREEN TAKES THE LEAD ROLE
annotations.story: Puma's 1998 home shirt flipped the usual formula: dark green became Morocco's primary World Cup color for the first time, with a white-outlined red stripe slashed across the chest. The away kit, worn against Brazil, ran the opposite way — white base, bold red band, green trim beneath the collar.

annotations.label: A HOME CROWD ON FOREIGN SOIL
annotations.story: France's stadiums turned red and green for every Morocco match, a product of the millions-strong Moroccan diaspora settled across the country since the 1960s. Fans in Saint-Étienne and Montpellier often outnumbered the actual home nation's supporters, giving the Atlas Lions something close to a home tournament abroad.

1998.homeColors: red shirt, green shorts, red socks (Puma)

2018.headline: Cruel Own-Goal Opener

annotations.label: LATEST OWN GOAL IN WORLD CUP HISTORY
annotations.story: Substitute Aziz Bouhaddouz turned the ball into his own net in the fourth minute of stoppage time, handing Iran a 1–0 win in the tournament opener. It stood as the latest own goal ever to decide a World Cup match at the time, a cruel start to Morocco's return after 20 years away.

annotations.label: DELAYED TO BEAT THE FAKES
annotations.story: Adidas built the 2018 kit off its Tabela 18 template, a red home shirt with white cuffs and a faint pinstripe. The federation and Adidas deliberately delayed its public unveiling as long as possible, wary that Morocco's thriving counterfeit-shirt trade would flood markets with fakes before the real jerseys had even shipped.

annotations.label: TWO NATIONS, ONE COACH, BACK TO BACK
annotations.story: Hérvé Renard arrived in 2016 already the only coach to win the Africa Cup of Nations with two different countries, Zambia in 2012 and Ivory Coast in 2015. He built a third project in Morocco, guiding the Atlas Lions back to the World Cup after two decades away.

2018.homeColors: red shirt, green shorts, red socks (Adidas)

2022.headline: History-Making Run to Fourth

annotations.label: HAKIMI'S PANENKA SEALS IT
annotations.story: Down to the final round of the shootout, goalkeeper Yassine Bounou saved two Spanish penalties before Achraf Hakimi calmly chipped a Panenka down the middle to send Morocco through. It made them the first African nation to win a World Cup penalty shootout.

annotations.label: THE HEADER THAT TOPPLED PORTUGAL
annotations.story: Youssef En-Nesyri rose 2.78 meters off the ground to head in the only goal against Portugal, sending Cristiano Ronaldo and the pre-tournament favorites home. The header made Morocco the first African and first Arab team to reach a World Cup semi-final.

annotations.label: THREE MONTHS TO A SEMI-FINAL
annotations.story: Walid Regragui took the job barely ten weeks before kickoff, part of a federation shakeup nobody saw coming. He built team unity by flying players' mothers in for the tournament, telling them family belonged pitchside — an unusual call that turned a patched-together squad into a fourth-place finisher.

2022.homeColors: red shirt with green sleeve trim and star crest, black shorts, red socks (Puma)
2022.awayColors: white shirt with green zellige-pattern geometric graphics, white shorts (Puma)

2026.headline: Second Straight Quarter-Final
2026.homeColors: red shirt with green side panels and zellige-tile geometric patterns at the collar and cuffs, green shorts, red socks (Puma)
2026.awayColors: white shirt with a vertical Amazigh embroidery-inspired pattern and red-and-green trim, white shorts, white socks (Puma)

annotations.label: THE KEY MOMENT
annotations.story: Down 1-0 with the clock bleeding into stoppage time against the Netherlands, center-back Issa Diop rose to head home a 91st-minute equalizer that forced extra time. Yassine Bounou then saved Crysencio Summerville's spot kick, and Ismael Saibari buried the winning penalty, sending the Atlas Lions through 3-2 on shootouts.

annotations.label: THE KIT
annotations.story: Puma dressed the Atlas Lions in red with green side panels echoing the star on Morocco's flag, with geometric zellige-tile patterns replacing generic piping at the collar and cuffs. The white away shirt runs a vertical Amazigh embroidery motif down the chest, a nod to Morocco's Indigenous Berber heritage rarely rendered on a kit.

annotations.label: THE CONTEXT
annotations.story: Morocco's matches in Boston, Houston and New York doubled as reunions for a North American diaspora more than a million strong, echoing the red-and-green seas that turned French stadiums into home fixtures back in 1998. Walid Regragui, who built the 2022 semi-finalists in ten weeks, stayed on this cycle — rare continuity most federations never risk.
